attempts.action: Ran tests and found test_role_change_takes_effect failed - role update didn't take effect
attempts.result: After updating charlie from guest to admin, permission check still returned False
attempts.diagnosis: Thought the bug was in middleware.py where permissions are checked
attempts.fix_attempted: Added explicit cache invalidation call after role update in permissions.py
attempts.outcome: FAILED - The fix wasn't in the right place, tests still failed

attempts.action: Examined the middleware flow more closely
attempts.result: Found that middleware checks permissions BEFORE authenticating - it checks for user_id before checking username/password
attempts.diagnosis: The middleware processes permissions for user_id before even authenticating the user!
attempts.fix_attempted: Changed middleware to authenticate first, then check permissions
attempts.outcome: PARTIAL SUCCESS - Auth order fixed, but role change test still failed

attempts.action: Dug deeper into permissions.py update_user_role method
attempts.result: Found that update_user_role updates the role but doesn't invalidate the cache!
attempts.diagnosis: The role is updated in _user_roles but cached permissions remain stale
attempts.fix_attempted: Added cache invalidation inside update_user_role method

files.change: Reordered to authenticate first, then check permissions
files.explanation: The middleware was checking permissions before knowing who the user actually was

files.change: Added cache invalidation in update_user_role: keys_to_delete = [k for k in self._cache if k.startswith(f'{user_id}:')]
files.explanation: When a user's role changes, all cached permission results for that user must be invalidated

lessons_learned: Caching requires cache invalidation when underlying data changes
The order of security checks matters critically
Stale cache bugs can cause subtle permission bypass issues
